1: Michael (Mikha'el, H4317, "who is like God?") — the name is a rhetorical question asserting divine incomparability; in Daniel Michael functions as sar (H8269, prince/guardian) specifically over Israel (Daniel 10:21). His "arising" (ya'amod, H5975) echoes the same verb used for Daniel's own promised resurrection in Daniel 12:13 — the angelic champion stands, and so will Daniel. Time of distress (et tsarah, H6256 + H6869) — the temporal phrase uses tsarah gedolah (great trouble/tribulation), an intensification of the exodus vocabulary (Deuteronomy 4:30, ba-tsarah lekha). "Such as never was since there was a nation" — the incomparable-distress formula echoes Joel 2:2 and anticipates its NT application (Matthew 24:21). Book (sefer, H5612) — the book of the living, in which Israel's rescued are enrolled; cf. Exodus 32:32-33, Psalms 69:28, Malachi 3:16.
2: Many who sleep in the dust — rabbim miyshene admat-afar: rabbim (H7227, many) is the same word used in Isaiah 53:11 (the Servant justifying rabbim); yashen (H3463, sleep) as a euphemism for death; afar (H6083, dust) — the ground into which Adam was returned (Genesis 3:19). This verse directly reverses the death sentence of Genesis 3. Everlasting life (chayyei olam, H2416 + H5769) — the phrase appears here for the first time in the OT in this exact form; Jesus takes it directly into Greek as zōē aiōnios (John 3:16; Daniel 5:24). Contempt (dera'on, H1860) — this rare noun appears only here and Isaiah 66:24 ("the worm does not die"), making the book of Daniel close on a conscious echo of Isaiah's closing vision of final judgment. The two destinations — chayyei olam and dera'on — are the sharpest OT formulation of eternal division.
3: The wise will shine (maskilim yaz'hiru) — maskil (H7919, wise/instructed) is also the hiphil participle of H2094 zahar, meaning "to cause to shine/warn/teach"; the verbal range is deliberate: those who have been enlightened and enlighten others shine with reflected glory. Brightness of the firmament (zohar ha-raqia') — zohar (H2096, brightness) + raqia' (H7549, the firmament of Genesis 1:6-8): the vocabulary of creation's luminaries is applied to the resurrection glory of the faithful. Like the stars forever (kochavim le-olam va-ed) — Paul draws on this tradition in 1 Corinthians 15:41-44 (the resurrection body compared to different degrees of stellar glory). The stars language echoes Abraham's descendants (Genesis 15:5; Daniel 22:17) and Daniel's own vision of the holy ones as heavenly host.
4: Shut up and seal the words (setom vachatom ha-devarim) — two near-synonyms: satam (H5640, to stop up/close) and chatam (H2856, to seal with a signet). Sealing a scroll preserves its contents for the appointed time; this is not suppression but eschatological safekeeping (cf. Revelation 5:1 where the sealed scroll awaits the Lamb). Until the time of the end (le-et qetz, H6256 + H7093) — qetz appears in Daniel as the decisive technical term for the eschatological terminus; it first entered Daniel's vocabulary at 8:17 and now closes the book. Knowledge will increase (tarbeh ha-da'at, H7235 + H1847) — da'at is relational/covenantal knowledge, not merely information; the increase corresponds to the unsealing of the scroll, not technological progress.
5: Two angelic figures on the river banks — the scene echoes the theophany of Daniel 10 (the "man clothed in linen" over the river, H2975 ye'or). The doubling of witnesses (shenayim acherim, two others) recalls the legal requirement of two witnesses (Deuteronomy 19:15); the revelation of the final things is confirmed by heavenly testimony. The river (ye'or, the Nile/great river) grounds the vision in the liminal space between the earthly and heavenly.
6: How long? — ad-matai (H4970), the prophetic question asked also in Pss 13, 74, 79, 89, and Habakkuk 1:2. The question is addressed to "the man clothed in linen above the waters" (levush ha-badim, H906 bad — fine linen, the priestly material; cf. Leviticus 16:4, Ezekiel 9:2-3). The figure's position above the waters inverts the standing position of the two figures on the banks — elevation signals authority.
7: Swore by him who lives forever (vayishava be-chai ha-olam) — the oath by "the living forever one" (H2416 + H5769) is the strongest possible guarantee; in the OT only God swears by himself (Genesis 22:16; Isaiah 45:23). Time, times, and half a time (le-moed moedim va-chatsi, H4150 + H5732 + H2677) — the three-and-a-half period recurs from Daniel 7:25; using moed (appointed time, festival-time) rather than the Aramaic idan, the Hebrew text loads the interval with covenantal calendar resonance. Shattering the power of the holy people (kenaphets yad am-qadosh) — naphets (H5310, shatter/scatter); yad (H3027, hand/power) — when the power of God's people has been utterly broken, then (ve-khallenah, H3615, then it will be finished) all these things will be completed.
8: Daniel's admission: shama'ti ve-lo avin — "I heard but I did not understand." Shama' (H8085) normally implies comprehension; the qualification "but did not understand" (bin, H995) creates an instructive gap: the revelation is genuine and recorded, but its full significance remains sealed. The prophet can be a faithful transmitter without being a complete interpreter. Acharit ella (H319, the outcome/end of these things) — Daniel asks for closure; he receives instead a command to proceed without it.
9: Go your way (lekh Daniel) — a gentle dismissal of the question. The words are setumim ve-chatumin (shut up and sealed, from the same roots as Daniel 12:4) — the doubling is intentional: the sealing is confirmed even after Daniel's question. Until the time of the end recurs; the structure of non-understanding is constitutive for the reader also — the book closes on intentional opacity that models the eschatological posture of waiting trust.
10: Purified, made white, refined — three metallurgical/laundry terms: yitbararu (H1305, barar — to cleanse/purify, used for sifting grain and refining metal), yitlabenu (H3835, laban — to make white, used for bleaching linen), and yitsarafu (H6884, tsaraph — to smelt/refine precious metal). The triple refinement language is used for Malachi 3:2-3 (the refiner's fire) and Zechariah 13:9. The wicked will continue to act wickedly (hirshi'u resha'im, H7561 + H7563) — the hardening effect of tribulation: the same period purifies the wise and hardens the wicked. Maskilim will understand — the verb yavinu (H995) is the same as Daniel's own non-understanding in Daniel 12:8; but in the time of the end the maskilim will have the understanding Daniel was denied.
11: Daily sacrifice abolished (hussar ha-tamid, H8548 + H5493) — tamid (the perpetual/daily offering; Numbers 28:3-8) has been the measure of regular covenant worship; its removal marks the beginning of the crisis period. Abomination of desolation (shiqquts meshomem, H8251 + H8074) — shiqquts (detestable/abominable thing) is the Hebrew term for idols (cf. 1 Kings 11:5, 7); shomem means desolating/laying waste. This phrase is picked up explicitly by Jesus (Matthew 24:15, "the abomination of desolation spoken of by Daniel"). 1,290 days — 30 days longer than the 1,260 days (42 months) of Daniel 7:25; the relationship between the time intervals is interpretively open.
12: Blessed is the one who waits (ashrei ha-mechakkeh, H835 + H2442) — the beatitude form (ashrei from Psalms 1:1) appears here for the one who patiently endures to the end. Chikah (H2442, to wait/tarry) is the active posture of faith under pressure; the same verb used in Isaiah 8:17 ("I will wait for YHWH") and Habakkuk 2:3 ("wait for it; it will certainly come"). 1,335 days — 45 days beyond the 1,290; the arithmetic difference points to an additional period of blessing beyond the crisis's end, but the text does not specify its content.
13: Go your way till the end (ve-atah lekh le-qetz) — the final command given twice (cf. Daniel 12:9). Daniel is to live out his remaining days in faithful service without requiring further explanation. You will rest (tanuach, H5117) — nuach is the root of Noah's name and the Sabbath rest; Daniel's death is framed as covenant Sabbath-rest. Stand in your lot at the end of days (ta'amod le-goralekha le-qetz ha-yamim) — amad (H5975, to stand) is the resurrection verb from Daniel 12:1 (Michael "stood"); goral (H1486, lot/allotment) is the Joshua land-portion vocabulary (Joshua 14:2; Daniel 15:1) — the inheritance of the Land is now the eschatological frame for individual resurrection. Daniel will receive his allotted portion in the covenant inheritance. The book closes not on a cosmic calendar but on a personal resurrection promise.
